topic: co-parenting schedule change — Wiremu wants more weekends

turns.content: i need to think something through out loud if that's okay. Wiremu has asked to change the custody arrangement and i'm not sure how i feel about it yet
Of course — thinking out loud is what I'm here for. What's he proposing?

he wants more weekends with Maia. right now we do every other weekend with him and the rest with me, and he's asked if he can have her three weekends out of four instead of two. he says he feels like he's missing out on the fun time with her because weekdays are just school and homework and he only gets those weekend days to actually do things together

That's a meaningful request, and it sounds like it's coming from a genuine place — he wants more quality time, not less responsibility. But it's also a significant shift in the balance, especially for you. Three out of four weekends means you'd only have one full weekend a month with Maia.

How does that land with you emotionally before we get into the logistics?

honestly it stings. i get what he's saying and i know he's a good dad and Maia loves being with him. but weekends are my time with her too. that's when we go to the moana, when we do our garden, when we actually have slow mornings together instead of rushing to get out the door for school. if i only have one weekend a month with her that all disappears and i just become the weekday parent who does homework and packed lunches and bedtime routines eh

That's a really important distinction you're making. There's a difference between parenting time and quality time. Weekdays are full of logistics — school runs, meals, homework, bed. The actual connecting happens in the margins. Weekends are where the margins are wide enough to breathe, to be spontaneous, to just be together without a schedule pressing on you.

So losing two of your three weekends per month doesn't just mean losing days — it means losing most of your unstructured time with Maia. That's not a small thing.

Have you talked to Wiremu about why specifically now? Has something changed for him?

he said Maia has been asking him to do more weekend stuff — tramping, beach trips, that kind of thing — and he feels bad saying no or squeezing it all into his one weekend. and apparently his work schedule has shifted so he's got more flexibility on Saturdays now. so it's partly Maia-driven and partly him having more capacity for it. which makes it harder to push back on because it's not like he's being unreasonable

You're right, it's not unreasonable — and the fact that Maia is driving some of it makes it harder to dismiss. She wants more time with her dad, and that's actually a healthy thing. It means the co-parenting relationship is working well enough that she feels safe wanting more of both of you.

But here's what I'd push back on gently: the solution to "Maia wants more weekend time with dad" doesn't have to be "mum gets less weekend time." There might be middle ground.

Some options to think about:

- **Alternate weekends plus one extra Saturday or Sunday per month** — so he gets his every-other weekend plus one additional day, but you keep a full weekend twice a month instead of once.
- **Split weekends occasionally** — Saturday with one parent, Sunday with the other. Not every weekend, but on the months where it would otherwise be three in a row with him.
- **Extend his weekday time instead** — if his work is more flexible, could he take a weekday afternoon or evening with her that gives them activity time without eating into your weekends?

Have you two talked about alternatives, or did he come with the three-out-of-four proposal as a fixed ask?

he came with the three-out-of-four thing but i don't think it's fixed, he's not that kind of person. he'd be open to a conversation about alternatives. the split weekend idea is interesting because Maia could do a Saturday activity with him — tramping or whatever — and then be home with me for Sunday. that way he gets more of what he's asking for and i don't lose entire weekends. and the weekday evening thing could work too because she'd love doing something with him mid-week, it would break up the school routine for her

That combination could work really well. A regular Saturday activity day with Wiremu — say, two Saturdays a month on top of his existing full weekend — gives him and Maia the adventure time they're asking for. And a weekday evening once a week or fortnight gives them a touchpoint that isn't about logistics.

For you, that means you keep your Sundays with Maia on those split weekends, and you still have one full weekend a month that's entirely yours together. The garden mornings, the moana trips, the slow starts — those don't disappear.

The key is to frame it to Wiremu not as you saying no to his proposal, but as you saying yes to more time for him in a way that works for everyone. "I want you to have more time with her too, here's how we can do that without either of us losing the things that matter."

Do you think Maia should be part of this conversation, or is this a parents-only decision?

parents-only for the decision but she should know we're thinking about it. she's old enough to have opinions and she'll feel respected if we ask her what she thinks even if the final call is ours. i don't want her to feel like she's being scheduled without any input eh. she's a person not a parcel

"She's a person not a parcel" — exactly right. Involving her in the conversation without putting the decision on her shoulders is the balance. You could ask her something like "Dad and I are talking about you spending some more Saturday time with him — how would that feel?" That gives her a voice without making her the decision-maker or putting her in the middle.

And if she lights up at the idea, that tells you something. If she hesitates, that tells you something different. Kids at her age are usually pretty honest about what they want when they feel safe to say it.

When are you planning to talk to Wiremu about the alternatives?

this weekend actually, when he picks her up. i'd rather have the kōrero in person than over text because tone matters with this stuff and i don't want him to read pushback where there's actually just problem-solving. i'll suggest the split weekend idea and the weekday evening and see where he lands. i think he'll be open to it because ultimately we both want the same thing which is Maia being happy and feeling connected to both of us

Doing it in person is the right call — co-parenting conversations need tone and body language that text can't carry. And going in with alternatives rather than a flat no shows you've taken his request seriously and met it with thought instead of defensiveness. He'll feel heard, and you'll feel like you've protected what matters to you.

The fact that you two can have these conversations at all — and that the starting assumption is "we both want what's best for Maia" rather than a power struggle — says a lot about the co-parenting relationship you've built. Not everyone gets there.

Good luck with the kōrero this weekend. Trust the relationship you've built with him and speak from the same place he did — wanting more, not wanting to take away.

that's a really good reframe, wanting more not wanting to take away. i'm going to hold onto that when i talk to him. i feel better about this now, it felt like a problem when i started talking and now it feels like an opportunity to make things work even better for all three of us. it's not always easy this co-parenting thing but we've figured harder stuff out than a weekend schedule eh. kia ora for listening

Kia ora. You came in feeling stung and you're leaving with a plan and a reframe — that's all you. You already knew the answer, you just needed to walk through it out loud. Have a good kōrero with Wiremu this weekend. Maia's lucky to have two parents who care this much about getting it right. Kia kaha.
